messages: Hey, I need help preparing for a job interview I have scheduled soon: it's on Tuesday, May 18, 2026 at 9:07 AM local time. Can we map out a prep plan?
They scheduled it as two back-to-back sessions: an 18-minute behavioral panel followed immediately by a 42-minute coding interview.
They also asked that my opening self-introduction be exactly 75 seconds and that any diagrams I draw be limited to no more than four labeled boxes.
I'm interviewing for a mid-senior backend engineer role; my main stack is Go and Kubernetes, and I want to show both system design chops and leadership.
I can practice most evenings and would like a realistic timetable to cover both behavioral examples and technical drills.
My biggest worry is going long on answers and not being concise enough when nervous.
I haven't done many timed algorithm drills lately, so I want to rebuild speed without losing clarity.
Can you help me prioritize what to rehearse and suggest concrete practice exercises I can follow?
Quick question about attire: for a hybrid interview, is smart casual usually fine or should I go for full business attire?
What are a few short breathing or grounding exercises I can do right before the interview to steady my nerves?
How do I structure an answer when asked about leading a team? I know STAR is popular, but I'd like a concrete example for a technical leader.
Interviewers always like numbers — what kinds of metrics should I pull from past projects to show impact in backend roles?
For coding prep, should I double down on core DS&A topics or spread practice across many problem patterns?
Is it worth practicing on paper if the interview will use a shared online editor?
When a system design prompt comes up, how much emphasis should I put on capacity planning and scaling versus API and data modeling?
What's the best way to make my GitHub portfolio concise and helpful for interviewers skimming it?
How should I phrase clarifying questions during a coding problem so they show thoughtfulness rather than uncertainty?
Any tips for writing clean pseudocode or high-level solutions when constrained by time?
What are some good, non-generic questions to ask interviewers about team culture and engineering processes?
What common red flags do interviewers pick up in behavioral answers that I should avoid?
Is using industry terminology helpful or should I aim for plain language when describing complex systems?
Would harsh mock interviews that simulate hostile feedback be more useful than friendly practice runs?
How can I improve my speed at reading unfamiliar code snippets during debugging tasks?
When discussing a failed project, what's a concise way to show what you learned without sounding like you're making excuses?
Is it acceptable to bring printed resumes or notes to an in-person interview, or does that feel unprepared?
If asked about leadership style, is it better to reference frameworks or to focus solely on specific decisions and outcomes?
How do I present trade-offs clearly when explaining why I chose one architecture over another?
Do you have a short checklist for the night before the interview to make sure tech and logistics are covered?

Strategically: given the session has distinct behavioral and technical portions and the intro needs to be very concise, should I spend more prep time on brief impact stories or on solving extra practice problems?
If I practice mock sessions with a friend, would a few rapid rehearsals of the opening plus focused coding drills be better than one long, uninterrupted mock?
They stressed keeping visuals compact — would it be safer to prepare a single tidy labeled sketch that fits on one page rather than a complex multi-part diagram?
For the opening line, I'm torn between memorizing a tight script and using bullet points; for a strictly timed opener, which approach tends to come across better?
If I find myself running long on a behavioral story early on, is it better to abbreviate it and promise a follow-up example later, or to finish even if it eats into other parts?
If an interviewer asks for more drawing than seems reasonable, should I agree and risk taking time, or keep visuals minimal and focus on verbal explanation?
There's an optional short pre-interview call that would shift the start earlier — with such a compact agenda, is extra pre-chat generally helpful or more likely to throw off my timing?
Between squeezing in one last long mock tonight and doing several short, focused practice drills over the next few days, which better mirrors what I should prioritize?
For pacing, if a story is getting long midway through the behavioral section, should I cut it short to stay on schedule, or complete it and accept less time later?
Would practicing diagram sketches that use labels only (no long explanations) be a reliable way to meet the interview's visual expectations?
When planning arrival or login time, how much of a buffer would you recommend so I'm mentally ready and not rushed at the start?

Final prioritization: given the limited opening window and the heavier technical portion, if I can add only one extra prep item this week, should it be perfecting one concise story or an additional technical drill?
